tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
 - timer_start(int): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_1>>
 - timer_stop(int): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_2>>
 - unknown: 4 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_3>>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - IDIV: 2 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
 - VZEROUPPER: 2 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_2>>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.
hint.title: Conversion instructions
Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This function is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

The binary function is loading 112 bytes.

load    : 16%
store   : 18%
mul     : NA (no mul vectorizable/vectorized instructions)

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 32.50 cycles. At this rate:
 - 1% of peak load performance is reached (3.45 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 4% of peak store performance is reached (6.15 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 32.50 to 25.63 cycles (1.27x speedup).

header:
Warnings:
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(,%R14,8),%RAX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%RAX,%RDX,1),%R9D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	0x1(%RDX),%R10D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x1(%R13),%R11D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(,%R8,8),%RAX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x2(%R13),%R9D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	0x8(,%R10,8),%RCX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%R10,%RDI,1),%RSI] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x1(%R9),%ECX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(,%RSI,8),%R8] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x2(%RCX),%R9D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%RBX,%R9,8),%RCX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%RBX,%R13,8),%RDX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%RBX,%RAX,8),%RSI] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%R14,%RAX,1),%R14] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%R14,%R10,1),%R14] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x1(%R15),%R13D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x28(%RBP),%RSP] is unknown
 - Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions.
Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis  or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 48.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Make array accesses unit-stride:
  * If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is probably not vectorized.
Only 18% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 32.50 to 4.57 cycles (7.12x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.
